allusions.surface: We have heard in the fables of Eriphyle ... a necklace ... she betrayed her husband's life
allusions.suspected_source: Greek mythological tradition (cf. Sophocles, Eriphyle; Ennius, Eriphyla)
allusions.translator_note: The Eriphyle figure was familiar from Greek tragedy, including Sophocles's lost play; treated by Ennius as well. Cicero may have any of several intermediaries in mind.

allusions.surface: famous bull which Phalaris ... is said to have had
allusions.translator_note: Phalaris's bull was first commemorated in Pindar; by Cicero's day a commonplace of cruelty-tradition. Cicero's report of Scipio Aemilianus's mot on returning the bull may be from Polybius.

allusions.surface: famous Greek epigram was carved on the base
allusions.translator_note: Cicero records that the empty base "declares what was there," but does not quote the epigram. The original is lost.
Cicero glosses Iuppiter Imperator as Greek Zeus Ourios ("granter of fair winds"), the cult title at the Bosphoran temple. This is informational rather than allusive.
